subject: ap-chemistry
title: CS₂ + Cl₂ Reaction: Stoichiometry, Kinetics & Bonding
stimulus:
Carbon tetrachloride, CCl$_4$, can be synthesized according to the reaction:

CS$_2$(g) + 3 Cl$_2$(g) → CCl$_4$(g) + S$_2$Cl$_2$(g)

A chemist runs the reaction at a constant temperature of 120°C in a rigid 25.0 L container.

parts.prompt:
Chlorine gas, Cl$_2$(g), is initially present in the container at a pressure of 0.40 atm.
(i) How many moles of Cl$_2$(g) are in the container?
(ii) How many grams of carbon disulfide, CS$_2$(g), are needed to react completely with the Cl$_2$(g)?
parts.scoring_notes: Part (a)(i): Container is 25.0 L at 120°C (393 K). n(Cl$_2$) = PV/RT = (0.40)(25.0)/[(0.08206)(393)] = 0.31 mol. Part (a)(ii): From the balanced equation CS$_2$ + 3 Cl$_2$ → CCl$_4$ + S$_2$Cl$_2$, the mole ratio CS$_2$:Cl$_2$ = 1:3. Mass CS$_2$ = 0.31 × (1/3) × 76.13 = 7.9 g.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of Cl$_2$ using the ideal gas law: 1 point

required_elements: $n = PV/RT$ using P = 0.40 atm, the given volume, and the given/implied temperature.
Correct numerical answer consistent with the container dimensions.
alternatives.correct_example: $n(\text{Cl}_2) = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{(0.40\text{ atm})(V\text{ L})}{(0.08206\text{ L·atm/mol·K})(T\text{ K})}$. Substitute the container volume and temperature from the stimulus.

wrong_examples: Using P in Pa or T in °C — must convert to atm and K.
common_traps: PV = nRT: n = PV/(RT). T must be in Kelvin.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct answer with supporting work.

sample_responses.commentary: CB model response: applies $PV = nRT$ with $T = 120$°C $+ 273 = 393$ K, obtains 0.31 mol Cl$_2$ with correct units.
Uses temperature in °C instead of K; $PV = nRT$ requires the absolute temperature.

scoring_points.description: For correct mass of CS$_2$ using 1:3 mole ratio: 1 point

required_elements: CS$_2$ : Cl$_2$ = 1 : 3 (from balanced equation CS$_2$ + 3 Cl$_2$ → CCl$_4$ + S$_2$Cl$_2$).
alternatives.correct_example: From CS$_2$ + 3 Cl$_2$ → CCl$_4$ + S$_2$Cl$_2$: moles CS$_2$ = moles Cl$_2$ / 3. Mass CS$_2$ = $0.31 \times \tfrac{1}{3} \times 76.13 = 7.9$ g.

wrong_examples: Using a 1:2 or 1:1 ratio — the balanced equation requires 3 mol Cl$_2$ per 1 mol CS$_2$.
common_traps: Balanced equation: CS$_2$ + 3 Cl$_2$ → CCl$_4$ + S$_2$Cl$_2$. The 1:3 stoichiometry is key.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for using the correct mole ratio (may be implicit). 1 point is earned for the mass of CS$_2$.

sample_responses.response: $0.31\text{ mol Cl}_2 \times \dfrac{1\text{ mol CS}_2}{3\text{ mol Cl}_2} \times \dfrac{76.13\text{ g CS}_2}{1\text{ mol CS}_2} = 7.9$ g CS$_2$
sample_responses.commentary: CB model response: applies the 1:3 mole ratio from the balanced equation, converts moles of CS$_2$ to grams using the molar mass (76.13 g/mol).

sample_responses.response: $0.31\text{ mol Cl}_2 \times \frac{1\text{ mol CS}_2}{1\text{ mol Cl}_2} \times 76.13 = 23.6\text{ g CS}_2$
sample_responses.commentary: Uses an incorrect 1:1 mole ratio; the balanced equation requires 3 moles of Cl$_2$ per mole of CS$_2$.

parts.prompt:
At 30°C the reaction is thermodynamically favorable, but no reaction is observed to occur. However, at
120°C, the reaction occurs at an observable rate.
(i) Explain how the higher temperature affects the collisions between the reactant molecules so that the
reaction occurs at an observable rate at 120°C.
(ii) The graph below shows a distribution for the collision energies of reactant molecules at 120°C. Draw
a second curve on the graph that shows the distribution for the collision energies of reactant molecules
at 30°C.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (b)(i): At higher temperature, molecules have greater average kinetic energy. A larger fraction of collisions have energy ≥ E$_a$ (the activation energy), so more collisions are effective and the observable reaction rate increases. Part (b)(ii): Lower temperature curve: same total area (same number of molecules), shifted left (lower average energy), taller peak, broader at low energies but lower fraction above E$_a$.

parts.prompt:
S$_2$Cl$_2$ is a product of the reaction.
(i) In the box below, complete the Lewis electron-dot diagram for the S$_2$Cl$_2$ molecule by drawing in all of
the electron pairs.
(ii) What is the approximate value of the Cl−S−S bond angle in the S$_2$Cl$_2$ molecule that you drew in
part (c)(i)?
parts.scoring_notes: Part (c)(i): S$_2$Cl$_2$ has the connectivity Cl–S–S–Cl. Each S has: 1 bond to Cl, 1 bond to S, and 2 lone pairs (10 electrons per S, using expanded octet; or each S has 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs). All lone pairs must be shown. Part (c)(ii): Each S has 2 bonding pairs + 2 lone pairs = 4 electron domains (bent/tetrahedral geometry). Cl–S–S angle ≈ 103° (less than 109.5° due to lone pair repulsion). Accept 100–108°.

parts.prompt:
CCl$_4$(g) can also be produced by reacting CHCl$_3$(g) with Cl$_2$(g) at 400°C, as represented by the equation
below.
CHCl$_3$(g) + Cl$_2$(g) → CCl$_4$(g) + HCl(g)
At the completion of the reaction a chemist successfully separates the CCl$_4$(g) from the HCl(g) by cooling
the mixture to 70°C, at which temperature the CCl$_4$(g) condenses while the HCl(g) remains in the gaseous
state.
(i) Identify all types of intermolecular forces present in HCl(l).
(ii) What can be inferred about the relative strengths of the intermolecular forces in CCl$_4$(l) and HCl(l)?
Justify your answer in terms of the information above.
parts.scoring_notes: Part (d)(i): HCl(l) has dipole–dipole forces (HCl is polar, E.N. difference creates permanent dipole) and London dispersion forces. Accept also listing hydrogen bonding if the student recognizes Cl can weakly H-bond — though HCl does not have strong H-bonding compared to HF; primary forces are dipole–dipole and LDF. Part (d)(ii): CCl$_4$(l) has stronger IMFs than HCl(g) at 70°C — CCl$_4$ condenses (becomes liquid) while HCl remains gas. A substance is liquid when intermolecular forces are strong enough to hold molecules together at that temperature. Since CCl$_4$ is liquid at 70°C but HCl is gas, CCl$_4$ must have stronger IMFs (even though CCl$_4$ is nonpolar, its larger, more polarizable electron cloud gives it stronger LDFs than HCl).

scoring_points.description: For identifying dipole–dipole forces and LDFs in HCl(l): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: HCl(l) exhibits dipole–dipole forces (HCl has a permanent dipole due to the large electronegativity difference between H and Cl) and London dispersion forces (all molecules have LDFs).

wrong_examples: Hydrogen bonding — H bonded to Cl is not strongly hydrogen-bonded (only H bonded to N, O, or F creates true H-bonds).
common_traps: Hydrogen bonding is strong only with H–F, H–O, H–N. H–Cl is polar (dipole–dipole) but not a strong hydrogen bond donor.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for both types of forces: dipole–dipole forces AND London dispersion forces.

sample_responses.response: Dipole–dipole forces and London dispersion forces.
sample_responses.commentary: Both required IMFs present in HCl(l) are correctly named — dipole–dipole (from the H–Cl polar bond) and LDF (present in all molecules) — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Hydrogen bonding and London dispersion forces.
sample_responses.commentary: HCl does not exhibit hydrogen bonding as the dominant IMF; the classic hydrogen-bonding criterion requires H bonded directly to N, O, or F. HCl's primary polar IMF is dipole–dipole, not H-bonding.

scoring_points.description: For CCl$_4$ has stronger IMFs than HCl at 70°C with condensation reasoning: 1 point

required_elements: CCl$_4$ has stronger IMFs than HCl.
CCl$_4$ condenses at 70°C (becomes liquid) while HCl remains a gas — substances with stronger IMFs have higher boiling points.
alternatives.correct_example: The IMFs in CCl$_4$(l) must be stronger than those in HCl at 70°C. At 70°C, CCl$_4$ is a liquid (it condenses), meaning its IMFs are strong enough to hold molecules together. HCl remains a gas at 70°C, meaning its IMFs are weaker and insufficient to condense it at that temperature. Therefore, CCl$_4$ has stronger IMFs than HCl.

wrong_examples: Saying HCl has stronger IMFs — HCl is a gas at 70°C, indicating weaker IMFs than CCl$_4$ which is a liquid.
common_traps: If a substance is a liquid at a given temperature, its IMFs are strong enough to maintain the condensed phase. Gas = weaker IMFs for that temperature.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct answer with a valid justification: the intermolecular forces among CCl$_4$ molecules must be stronger than those among HCl molecules because CCl$_4$ condenses at a higher temperature than HCl.

sample_responses.response: The IMFs in CCl$_4$(l) must be stronger than those in HCl(l) because CCl$_4$ condenses at 70°C (higher temperature) while HCl remains a gas at that temperature. Stronger IMFs require more thermal energy to overcome — CCl$_4$'s larger electron cloud gives it strong LDFs that outweigh HCl's dipole–dipole + LDF.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly infers stronger IMFs in CCl$_4$ from the phase-at-70°C observation — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: HCl has stronger IMFs because it is polar, but CCl$_4$ is not polar.
sample_responses.commentary: Polarity alone does not determine IMF strength — CCl$_4$ has far more electrons and a larger polarizable electron cloud, giving it stronger London dispersion forces than HCl's dipole–dipole + LDF. The condensation evidence at 70°C is definitive.
